docs.title: Personal Knowledge Docs
docs.text:
This site is the curated layer of a personal knowledge system for reusable engineering, delivery, documentation, systems, tools, templates, and sanitized lessons learned.

It is not a raw notes dump. Rough thinking starts in notes/, useful material is refined, and only durable public-safe knowledge is promoted into docs/ for publishing.

docs.title: Use This Site For
docs.text:
• Starting a new project with a tested process
  • Choosing a stack or tool category
  • Reusing templates, checklists, and standards
  • Pulling lessons from previous experience
  • Keeping project delivery consistent

docs.title: Main Entry Points
docs.text:
• Start Here: project-start workflows and common paths
  • Knowledge Map: full index of the knowledge system
  • Templates: reusable documents and checklists
  • Decisions: stack and architecture decision guides
  • Documentation Standards: writing and publishing rules
  • Engineering Playbook: engineering categories and tool layers

docs.title: Operating Model
docs.text:
Obsidian capture -> notes/ working area -> docs/ curated content -> MkDocs Material -> hosted static site

The published site is intentionally curated so it stays useful under pressure.

docs.title: Knowledge Map
docs.text: This is the main retrieval map for the knowledge base.

docs.title: Start Here
docs.text: Use this page when starting or shaping a project.

docs.title: New Project Flow
docs.text:
1. Define the project using the Project Brief.
  2. Pick a default stack from Best Free MVP Stack or Recommended Client Project Stack.
  3. Confirm major choices with the decision guides:
  4. Choose Auth
  5. Choose Database
  6. Choose Hosting
  7. Choose Observability
  8. Write the technical shape with the Technical Specification.
  9. Apply File Structure Standards.
  10. Apply Documentation Standards.
  11. Track architecture choices with the ADR Template.
  12. Prepare launch with the Deployment Checklist.
  13. Close delivery with the Client Handover Checklist.

docs.title: Promotion Flow
docs.text:
Capture first, publish later:

notes/inbox -> notes/drafts or notes/working -> docs/ curated page -> MkDocs site

Only promote material into docs/ when it is reusable, public-safe, and clear enough to help future work.

docs.title: Link Rule
docs.text: Use standard Markdown links in docs/. Obsidian wiki-links can be used in notes/, but normalize them when promoting content.

docs.title: Tags
docs.text: Use tags to find related reusable material across the knowledge base.

docs.title: writing
docs.text: • Documentation Standards

docs.title: Decisions
docs.text:
Decision docs turn raw tool knowledge into reusable judgment.

Use them when starting a new project, revisiting an architecture, or explaining why a tool belongs in a stack.

docs.title: Stack Decisions
docs.text:
• Choose Auth
  • Choose Database
  • Choose Hosting
  • Choose Observability
  • Choose AI App Stack

docs.title: Opinionated Defaults
docs.text:
• Best Free MVP Stack
  • Recommended Client Project Stack

docs.title: Decision Rule
docs.text: Prefer the boring, maintainable default unless the project has a clear reason to optimize for something else.

docs.title: Default Stack
docs.text:
• Frontend: Vercel or Cloudflare Pages
  • Backend: Supabase
  • Database: Postgres
  • Auth: Supabase Auth
  • Storage: Supabase Storage when needed
  • CI/CD: GitHub Actions
  • Monitoring: Sentry
  • Linting and formatting: Biome
  • Documentation: MkDocs Material or README-first Markdown

docs.title: Use This When
docs.text:
• Speed matters more than custom infrastructure
  • The app is early-stage
  • The team is small
  • The budget is tight
  • The system can tolerate managed-platform defaults

docs.title: Upgrade Triggers
docs.text:
• Free-tier limits become operational risk
  • Background work needs stronger guarantees
  • The app needs enterprise auth
  • Compliance or data residency changes the hosting decision

docs.title: Default Recommendation
docs.text:
Start with a normal web app stack and add AI only where it creates clear user value.

Use hosted models first. Add vector search or agent frameworks only after the product flow proves it needs them.

docs.title: Common Stack
docs.text:
• Frontend: Next.js or React
  • Backend: serverless functions or API routes
  • Database: Postgres
  • Embeddings: pgvector for simple retrieval
  • Observability: Sentry plus model usage logging
  • Evaluation: small test sets before complex eval tooling

docs.title: Avoid
docs.text:
• Building RAG before documents and retrieval needs are clear
  • Adding agent frameworks for simple prompt-response flows
  • Storing prompts, outputs, and costs nowhere

docs.title: Future Default
docs.text: Static docs and structured Markdown first. AI retrieval comes later when the knowledge base has enough useful content.

docs.title: Default Recommendation
docs.text:
Use Supabase Auth when the project already uses Supabase or Postgres and needs fast MVP auth.

Use Clerk when the app needs polished auth UI quickly and vendor lock-in is acceptable.

Use WorkOS or Auth0 when enterprise SSO, SCIM, or B2B identity is a real requirement.

docs.title: Choose Based On
docs.text:
• Required login methods
  • B2B or B2C audience
  • SSO and organization support
  • User management UI requirements
  • Self-hosting requirements
  • Cost at expected scale
  • Framework fit

docs.title: Avoid
docs.text:
• Building custom auth for a normal MVP
  • Choosing enterprise auth for a simple app
  • Ignoring account recovery, email verification, and session management

docs.title: Future Default
docs.text: For most small apps: Supabase Auth or Clerk.

docs.title: Default Recommendation
docs.text:
Use Postgres unless there is a specific reason not to.

For MVPs, Supabase is the fastest full-platform Postgres default. For serverless Postgres without the full backend platform, use Neon.

docs.title: Choose Based On
docs.text:
• Relational data needs
  • Query complexity
  • Hosting model
  • Local development ergonomics
  • Migrations and backups
  • Team familiarity
  • Expected scale

docs.title: Common Defaults
docs.text:
• Most apps: Postgres
  • Local-first or embedded: SQLite
  • Analytics on files: DuckDB
  • Edge SQLite: Turso
  • Document-heavy flexible schema: MongoDB
  • Embeddings in app data: Postgres with pgvector

docs.title: Avoid
docs.text:
• Choosing NoSQL to avoid schema design
  • Adding a separate vector database before Postgres with pgvector is insufficient
  • Picking a database only because the free tier is attractive

docs.title: Default Recommendation
docs.text:
Use GitHub Pages for static documentation sites.

Use Vercel for frontend-heavy Next.js apps.

Use Cloudflare Pages for static or edge-first apps.

Use Render, Railway, Fly.io, or Cloud Run when the app needs a real backend service.

docs.title: Choose Based On
docs.text:
• Static site, server-rendered app, API, worker, or container
  • Build and deploy complexity
  • Preview deployment needs
  • Environment variable model
  • Background jobs
  • Runtime limits
  • Domain and DNS control

docs.title: Avoid
docs.text:
• Hosting long-running jobs on platforms optimized for short serverless requests
  • Adding containers before the app needs them
  • Choosing a platform without checking production logs and rollback workflow

docs.title: Default Recommendation
docs.text:
Use Sentry as the default first production observability tool for app errors.

Add uptime checks, structured logs, and metrics when the app has real users or operational risk.

docs.title: Minimum Production Baseline
docs.text:
• Error tracking
  • Uptime monitoring
  • Deployment visibility
  • Basic logs
  • Alert destination
  • Known owner for alerts

docs.title: Tool Defaults
docs.text:
• Error tracking: Sentry
  • Metrics: Prometheus and Grafana
  • Tracing: OpenTelemetry
  • Logs: platform logs first, Loki or Better Stack when needed

docs.title: Avoid
docs.text:
• Shipping production without error visibility
  • Adding a complex observability stack before there is operational need
  • Alerts that nobody owns

docs.title: Default Recommendation
docs.text:
Use maintainable, well-known tools with low handover risk.

For typical client web projects:

  • Frontend: Next.js or static React depending on requirements
  • Hosting: Vercel, Cloudflare Pages, or GitHub Pages for docs/static
  • Database: Supabase Postgres when a backend is needed
  • Auth: Supabase Auth or Clerk
  • Payments: Stripe
  • Email: Resend
  • Monitoring: Sentry
  • CI/CD: GitHub Actions
  • Docs: Markdown in repo plus MkDocs when needed

docs.title: Prioritize
docs.text:
• Clear ownership
  • Simple deploys
  • Easy rollback
  • Good documentation
  • Low support burden
  • Handover-friendly tooling

docs.title: Avoid
docs.text:
• Novel tools without client benefit
  • Over-custom infrastructure
  • Tool choices that only the original developer can operate

docs.title: Delivery Standards
docs.text: Delivery standards make projects easier to start, review, launch, and hand over.

docs.title: Baseline
docs.text:
Every meaningful project should have:

  • Project brief
  • Technical specification or implementation plan
  • Setup instructions
  • Environment variable documentation
  • Deployment path
  • Ownership and access notes
  • Launch checklist
  • Handover notes

docs.title: Delivery Principle
docs.text: Every project should be understandable without asking the original builder to remember hidden context.

docs.title: Handover Minimum
docs.text:
• Repository access
  • Production access
  • Domain/DNS ownership
  • Environment variable list without secret values
  • Deployment instructions
  • Known risks
  • Maintenance tasks
  • Support contacts

docs.title: Documentation Standards
docs.text: Documentation should make future work faster and safer.

docs.title: Published Page Requirements
docs.text:
Every page in docs/ should have:

  • A clear title
  • Minimal frontmatter
  • A specific purpose
  • Public-safe content
  • Useful links to related pages
  • Standard Markdown links

Default frontmatter:

---
title: Page Title
status: draft
tags: [documentation, tooling]
updated: 2026-05-04
---

docs.title: Status Values
docs.text:
• draft: useful but still evolving
  • active: current working guidance
  • evergreen: durable reference
  • archived: kept for context, not current guidance

docs.title: Writing Rules
docs.text:
• Prefer reusable guidance over stream-of-consciousness notes.
  • Keep raw capture in notes/.
  • Promote only clear and public-safe material into docs/.
  • Use examples when they reduce ambiguity.
  • Keep decisions separate from tool indexes.
  • Avoid private names, credentials, client secrets, and sensitive context.

docs.title: Link Rules
docs.text:
• Use standard Markdown links in docs/.
  • Wiki-links are allowed in notes/.
  • Normalize important links when promoting content.

docs.title: File Structure Standards
docs.text: Use predictable file structures so future projects are easier to scan, hand over, and automate.

docs.title: Documentation Repo Pattern
docs.text:
docs/       Published documentation
notes/      Working notes and drafts
templates/  Raw reusable templates

docs.title: Application Repo Pattern
docs.text:
.
+-- README.md
+-- docs/
+-- src/
+-- tests/
+-- scripts/
+-- .github/
+-- .env.example

docs.title: Naming Rules
docs.text:
• Use lowercase kebab-case for Markdown files.
  • Name files by purpose, not date, unless the page is an event log.
  • Prefer index.md for section landing pages.
  • Keep templates in a predictable templates/ folder.

docs.title: Minimum Project Docs
docs.text:
• README.md
  • docs/setup.md
  • docs/deployment.md
  • docs/architecture.md or ADRs
  • .env.example

docs.title: Engineering Playbook
docs.text: A living reference of software engineering tools, categorized by system layer. Use this as lookup, comparison, and decision-support material.

docs.title: How To Use This
docs.text:
For any project, answer:

  1. Auth: which provider?
  2. Data: which database?
  3. Compute: serverless, container, edge, or static?
  4. Async: queues, workflows, or scheduled jobs?
  5. Delivery: CI/CD and hosting?
  6. Observability: logs, metrics, traces, and errors?
  7. AI: where does it actually fit?

docs.title: Version Control And Collaboration
docs.text:
• Git: foundational distributed version control
  • GitHub: Actions, pull requests, ecosystem
  • GitLab: full DevOps platform
  • Bitbucket: Atlassian integration
  • Perforce: large binary repos
  • Gitea: lightweight self-hosted Git
  • Graphite: stacked diffs workflow

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Prefer decision guides over raw lists when starting real projects. Use this playbook to discover options, then use the decision documents to choose.

docs.title: Experience Lessons
docs.text:
This section turns previous experience into reusable judgment.

It is for sanitized lessons and patterns, not diary-style notes or private internal context.

docs.title: Lesson Structure
docs.text:
Use this shape for each lesson:

# Lesson Title

## Context Pattern

## Problem Type

## What Worked

## What Failed

## Default Recommendation

## Reusable Takeaway

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
• Remove client secrets and private business details.
  • Avoid identifiable internal conflicts.
  • Keep emotional/raw notes in private spaces outside published docs.
  • Focus on reusable patterns, decision defaults, and warning signs.

docs.title: Knowledge Workflow
docs.text: This repo separates capture, curation, and publishing.

docs.title: Capture
docs.text:
Put raw thoughts, links, working ideas, and incomplete research in notes/.

Recommended folders:

  • notes/inbox/: quick capture
  • notes/drafts/: rough notes becoming real pages
  • notes/research/: research material and source notes
  • notes/working/: active thinking and project-specific drafts

docs.title: Curate
docs.text:
When a note becomes reusable:

  1. Remove private or temporary details.
  2. Add a stable title.
  3. Add minimal frontmatter.
  4. Convert important wiki-links to Markdown links.
  5. Move or rewrite it into docs/.

docs.title: Publish
docs.text: MkDocs builds only from docs/. This keeps the public site curated and protects rough working notes from accidental publication.

docs.title: Maintain
docs.text:
Review pages when:

  • A tool changes pricing or free-tier limits.
  • A project exposes a new lesson.
  • A checklist misses something important.
  • A template gets reused and needs tightening.

docs.title: Templates
docs.text:
Templates are first-class outputs of this knowledge base. Use them to turn previous work into repeatable project starts, delivery standards, and operational checklists.

Raw copy-ready templates live in the root templates/ folder. This page explains when to use each one.

docs.title: Project Brief
docs.text:
Use before implementation starts. It captures goals, audience, constraints, scope, risks, and success criteria.

Template: templates/project-brief.md

docs.title: Technical Specification
docs.text:
Use once the project goal is clear and the implementation needs a decision-complete shape.

Template: templates/technical-specification.md

docs.title: Architecture Decision Record
docs.text:
Use when making a meaningful technical choice that future you will need to understand.

Template: templates/architecture-decision-record.md

docs.title: Project Setup Checklist
docs.text:
Use at the start of a repo to avoid missing basics like README, env docs, linting, deployment path, and issue tracking.

Template: templates/project-setup-checklist.md

docs.title: Deployment Checklist
docs.text:
Use before launch or production promotion.

Template: templates/deployment-checklist.md

docs.title: Client Handover Checklist
docs.text:
Use when closing a client delivery or handing a system to another operator.

Template: templates/client-handover-checklist.md

docs.title: Postmortem
docs.text:
Use after outages, delivery misses, production issues, or major lessons.

Template: templates/postmortem.md

docs.title: Tool Evaluation Scorecard
docs.text:
Use before adopting a new tool or replacing an existing one.

Template: templates/tool-evaluation-scorecard.md

docs.title: Free And Free-Tier Engineering Tools
docs.text:
A curated table of tools that are free, open-source, self-hostable, or useful on a free tier.

Ratings are directional. Choose based on use case, constraints, and operating cost, not only popularity.

docs.title: Auth And Identity
docs.text: Tool Type Free Tier Rating Best For Avoid When Supabase Auth Hosted Yes 5/5 MVPs, Postgres-native auth Complex enterprise SSO Clerk Hosted Yes 4/5 Fast UI and auth Strict self-hosting Firebase Auth Hosted Yes 4/5 Quick social login Avoiding lock-in Auth0 Hosted Limited 4/5 Enterprise SSO Cost-sensitive scale WorkOS Hosted Limited 5/5 B2B enterprise auth Small apps Auth.js Library Yes 4/5 Next.js apps Non-Next stacks Keycloak Self-hosted Yes 4/5 Enterprise IAM Simple apps Ory Self-hosted Yes 4/5 Modular identity infra Small teams

docs.title: Databases
docs.text: Tool Type Free Tier Rating Best For Avoid When PostgreSQL Self-hosted Yes 5/5 Most apps Rarely SQLite Embedded Yes 4/5 Local-first apps Multi-user server systems Supabase Hosted Yes 5/5 Rapid backend Heavy custom backend logic Neon Hosted Yes 4/5 Serverless Postgres Strict latency control Turso Hosted Yes 5/5 Edge SQLite Heavy relational workloads DuckDB Embedded Yes 5/5 Local analytics OLTP apps MongoDB Atlas Hosted Yes 4/5 Flexible schema Strong relational needs Redis Self-hosted Yes 5/5 Cache and queues Durable primary storage Upstash Redis Hosted Yes 4/5 Serverless cache High-throughput workloads Meilisearch Self-hosted Yes 4/5 Quick search Complex analytics Typesense Self-hosted Yes 4/5 Fast search Large search clusters pgvector Extension Yes 5/5 Embeddings in SQL Massive vector scale Chroma Self-hosted Yes 4/5 Simple vector apps Enterprise scale

docs.title: Deployment And Hosting
docs.text: Tool Type Free Tier Rating Best For Avoid When Vercel Hosted Yes 5/5 Frontend and SSR Long-running jobs Netlify Hosted Yes 4/5 Static and JAMstack Backend-heavy apps Cloudflare Pages Hosted Yes 5/5 Edge and static apps Traditional backend apps GitHub Pages Hosted Yes 4/5 Static docs Dynamic apps Railway Hosted Limited 4/5 Quick fullstack deploys Strict free uptime Render Hosted Limited 4/5 Simple APIs Free tier sleeping Fly.io Hosted Limited 4/5 Geo apps Simple static sites

docs.title: CI/CD
docs.text: Tool Type Free Tier Rating Best For Avoid When GitHub Actions Hosted Yes 5/5 Most GitHub repos Complex enterprise pipelines GitLab CI/CD Hosted Yes 4/5 GitLab projects GitHub-first teams Jenkins Self-hosted Yes 2/5 Legacy systems Modern greenfield setups Dagger Open-source Yes 4/5 Pipelines as code Simple docs sites

docs.title: AI Coding And Agents
docs.text: Tool Type Free Tier Rating Best For Avoid When Cursor IDE Limited 5/5 Daily AI-assisted dev Offline-only work Cline Open-source Yes 4/5 Local agent workflows Plug-and-play UX Aider CLI Yes 4/5 Git-based coding GUI-first workflows Continue Open-source Yes 4/5 Custom AI coding setup Non-technical users Claude Code Tool Limited 5/5 Reasoning-heavy code tasks Cost-sensitive usage

docs.title: Observability
docs.text: Tool Type Free Tier Rating Best For Avoid When Sentry Hosted Yes 5/5 Error tracking Full APM replacement Prometheus Self-hosted Yes 5/5 Metrics Tiny apps Grafana Self-hosted Yes 5/5 Dashboards Quick hosted-only setup OpenTelemetry Framework Yes 5/5 Tracing standardization MVP complexity Loki Self-hosted Yes 4/5 Logs Enterprise managed logging Better Stack Hosted Limited 4/5 Uptime and logs Deep APM

docs.title: Recommended Free MVP Stack
docs.text:
• Frontend: Vercel or Cloudflare Pages
  • Backend: Supabase
  • Database: Postgres
  • Auth: Supabase Auth
  • Queue: BullMQ and Redis when needed
  • CI/CD: GitHub Actions
  • Monitoring: Sentry
  • Linting: Biome
  • Local AI: Ollama
  • Diagrams: Mermaid and diagrams.net

Keep this page reviewed because free tiers change over time.
